title: Impressions Up, Clicks Flat? Fix Your Amazon Main Image
persona: Kitchen-gadget founder selling a $40 electric kettle; Sponsored Products impressions climbing, clicks flat for three weeks.
angle: Priya's Amazon Ads dashboard shows impressions up 40% but clicks barely moved, and ACOS is creeping past breakeven, the classic invisible-in-the-grid problem. The coach runs generate_main_image_title_plan live on screen, turning her main image and title into one positioning statement (brand, real keyword, the one difference that matters) right at the amazon_main_image position where the scroll decision happens. The hook leans on fear_of_loss: she's paying to be seen by people who are clicking someone else instead.

title: Your Main Image and Title Are Fighting Each Other
persona: Supplement founder running magnesium gummies; category page CTR sits below the category median despite a redesigned thumbnail.
angle: Dana redesigned her thumbnail twice and CTR barely moved, because her new image promises 'calm' while her title still leads with a generic keyword, splitting the click decision in two. The coach runs generate_main_image_title_plan to force image and title into ONE positioning statement at the amazon_main_image position, with a CTR split-test plan to prove which version wins. The hook plays momentum: the fix is a same-day swap, not a redesign.

title: I Thought My Product Photo Was the Problem
persona: Pet-brand founder selling dog joint supplement chews; five-star reviews, but the listing gets buried below three lookalike competitors in the search grid.
angle: Marcus has the better product and the better reviews, but scrolling the search grid himself he can't find his own listing without searching by exact ASIN, his main image reads like every other bag of chews. He brings the coach the listing and runs generate_main_image_title_plan, which surfaces the one differentiator ('vet-formulated for senior joints') his current main image never says out loud. The hook is recognition: his dog's actual story was the click-worthy detail he'd been hiding in the bullet points instead of the image.

title: My Serum Had Great Reviews and Zero Clicks
persona: Beauty founder selling a vitamin C serum; 4.7-star rating, healthy repeat rate, but search-grid CTR under 0.3%.
angle: In review-style monologue, a composite founder explains that great reviews didn't matter because nobody was clicking through to read them, her main image looked identical to five competitors in the grid. Running generate_main_image_title_plan gave her one positioning statement (brand plus 'vitamin C' plus the specific difference: fermented, not synthetic) baked into image and title together. The hook plays identity: she wasn't selling to 'skincare people,' she was selling to people done with irritation, and the old image never said so.

title: I Tried On My Own Product to Find the Click
persona: Baby-gear founder selling a structured baby carrier; impressions solid from a viral TikTok mention, clicks didn't follow.
angle: On camera, wearing her own carrier, a composite founder holds her phone up beside her showing three main-image concepts from generate_main_image_title_plan, each testing a different first-80-character title angle. The hook plays belonging: the winning concept isn't the cleanest studio shot, it's the one showing exactly how the carrier sits on a body like the shopper's, since the coach's title formula flagged that 'ergonomic' alone wasn't the click driver, the specific hip-seat detail was.

title: Unboxing the One-Page Plan That Fixed Our CTR
persona: Outdoor-gear founder selling a portable camp chair; ACOS rising for two months straight despite no change in bids.
angle: A composite founder 'unboxes' the exported Main Image + Title Plan, literally opening the PDF on camera like a satisfying reveal, showing the before/after main image side by side and the title formula breakdown underneath. The hook plays momentum: ACOS climbed for two months because CPCs rose while clicks stayed flat, and the plan the coach generated is a same-week fix, not a redesign project.

title: We Reused Our Packshot as Our Meta Ad. Big Mistake.
persona: Coffee-subscription founder; boosted a Meta post using the exact Amazon packshot, huge reach, near-zero click-through.
angle: On screen, a composite founder shows her Ads Manager: 90,000 impressions, 38 clicks. She runs generate_main_image_title_plan through the coach not for Amazon this time, but because her paid social creative IS her Amazon packshot, the same broken positioning statement, just paying to show it to more people. The hook plays fear_of_loss: every dollar of that ad spend is buying impressions for an image that was already failing to earn a click.

title: Boosted the Post, Impressions Doubled, Clicks Didn't
persona: Fitness-equipment founder selling a resistance bands set; doubled ad spend on a boosted Instagram post, click volume unchanged.
angle: Show the problem first: a founder doubling ad spend on the same static creative, watching cost-per-click climb instead of clicks climb, a textbook rising-ACOS, flat-CTR trap. The coach diagnoses that her paid social creative is just her main image with a sticker on it, then runs generate_main_image_title_plan to rebuild the positioning statement (brand, 'resistance bands,' the difference: adjustable tension without buying five sets) before it goes back into the ad. The hook plays permission: she didn't need a video shoot, she needed the right eleven words.

title: My Ad Got Seen By Everyone, Clicked By No One
persona: Home-office founder selling a standing desk converter; a TikTok ad hit 200K views with a 0.4% click rate.
angle: A composite founder tells it like a confession: the ad went semi-viral, the DMs came in, but the actual click-through link sat untouched because the creative never made the specific case for why this converter beats the dozen others. Running generate_main_image_title_plan gave the coach a positioning statement built from her real customer language ('finally stand without buying a new desk') that became the ad's own hook. The hook plays identity: viral reach without a click is just noise with good production value.

title: Turns Out My Ad Was Selling the Wrong Thing
persona: Garage-brand founder selling a car detailing kit; a Facebook ad running for six weeks with declining CTR week over week.
angle: Review-style, a composite founder explains her ad's CTR dropped every week because the creative was selling 'premium detailing' when her buyers were googling 'fix my car before I sell it,' a mismatch generate_main_image_title_plan caught by testing her positioning statement against real search language. The hook plays recognition: the fix wasn't a new video, it was making the ad finally sound like her actual customer's problem instead of her product's features.

title: Same Image Format as Competitors, Half the Clicks
persona: Craft-supply founder selling an embroidery starter kit; image looks 'on-brand' next to competitors but underperforms them in CTR.
angle: Screen-recording style, a composite founder runs run_trust_gap on her listing and watches the IDEA scorecard flag Distinctive as her weakest pillar, since her main image is technically correct but forgettable at grid scale, indistinguishable from three other kits shoppers already scrolled past. The hook plays recognition: the score gives her the specific pillar to fix instead of another vague 'make it pop' note from a designer.

title: My Thumbnail Looked Fine. It Just Didn't Feel Safe.
persona: Sleep-brand founder selling a weighted blanket; CTR flat for months despite a professionally shot main image.
angle: A composite founder describes paying for a proper studio shoot and watching CTR not move at all, until run_trust_gap scored her listing and named Empathetic as the gap: a clean, cold product shot next to competitors' cozier, more human staging read as less trustworthy at a glance, not more premium. The hook plays belonging: shoppers scrolling for a weighted blanket are scrolling for comfort, and her 'professional' image was accidentally answering the wrong emotional question before anyone even clicked.

title: Your Skincare Ad Has Three Seconds to Earn Trust
persona: Skincare founder selling a retinol serum; paid social CTR under 0.5%, audience research confirms interest but not clicks.
angle: Problem first: a composite founder shows her audience targeting is right, the interest overlap is strong, but her ad still gets scrolled past because retinol buyers are naturally skeptical of claims and her creative reads like every other 'glass skin' ad. run_trust_gap scores the ad copy itself and flags Authentic as the gap, missing the specific 'I thought retinol would irritate me, but' skeptic language her real customers actually use. The hook plays permission: giving the skeptic in the audience the exact objection-answer they're waiting for.

title: Ranking Page One, Clicked Like Page Four
persona: Travel-gear founder selling packing cubes; a blog post ranks page one but organic CTR from the search results is far below average for that position.
angle: Review-style, a composite founder explains her post ranks well but the meta title and snippet don't make anyone want to click past the three listings above it, run_trust_gap applied to the content itself flags Insight-Driven as the gap, no specific proof point in the preview text. The hook plays fear_of_loss: a page-one ranking that nobody clicks is a wasted position, not a win.

title: My Blog Traffic Chart Looks Healthy. My Clicks Don't.
persona: Home-cleaning-brand founder running an SEO blog; impressions in Search Console climbing steadily, click-through rate declining for the same queries.
angle: Screen-share style, a composite founder pulls up Search Console showing impressions climbing month over month while CTR on her top queries quietly drops, a slow leak most founders never catch because the top-line traffic number still looks fine. The coach runs run_trust_gap against her ranking post and scores it weak on Distinctive, since three competing posts now say the exact same 'natural ingredients' claim in their snippets. The hook plays momentum: the fix is rewriting one line of meta copy, not another 1,500-word post.

title: Your Main Image Is Answering the Wrong Question
persona: Tech-accessory founder selling a phone camera lens kit; strong impressions from a broad keyword, click rate below 0.4%.
angle: Problem first: a composite founder's main image shows the lenses laid out neatly, accurate, professional, and completely un-clickable, because shoppers scrolling that keyword are asking 'will this actually look good on my Instagram,' not 'what's in the box.' The coach runs identify_decision_trigger and names recognition as the lever this purchase turns on: people buying this want to see themselves as a better photographer, not read a parts list. The hook plays recognition directly: the next main image shows the lens on a phone mid-shot, not off it.

title: I Kept Changing the Video. The Hook Was the Problem.
persona: Hydration-brand founder selling an insulated water bottle; four different video ad edits, all with similar low CTR.
angle: A composite founder admits she reshot the ad three times chasing better footage before the coach ran identify_decision_trigger and found the real lever was belonging, not the insulation specs she kept leading with, her buyers wanted to look like the kind of person who has their water bottle sorted, not learn about double-wall steel. The hook plays that exact trigger back at the viewer: 'everyone at the gym already has one, you're just not carrying it yet.'

title: My Ad Explained the Product. It Should've Explained Me.
persona: Gardening-brand founder selling raised garden bed kits; ad CTR strong in spring, collapsing by early summer with the same creative.
angle: Review-style, a composite founder explains her CTR seasonally tanked because her ad kept explaining bed dimensions when the coach's identify_decision_trigger run showed the actual lever was identity, buyers weren't shopping for a bed, they were shopping for the version of themselves who finally grows their own food this year. The hook plays identity: swap the specs-first hook for 'the garden you keep saying you'll start.'

title: Same Keyword, Same Rank, Half the Search Clicks
persona: Kids' STEM-toy founder running a blog targeting 'best building toys for 6 year olds'; ranking stable, click share declining as more competitors target the same query.
angle: Screen-recording, a composite founder watches identify_decision_trigger surface that parents searching this exact phrase are driven by fear_of_loss around screen time, not curiosity about toy features, while her meta title talks specs and three newer competitors' snippets speak directly to that anxiety. The hook plays fear_of_loss back at the searcher: 'before another rainy day turns into another tablet day.'

title: We Wrote the Right Post. We Picked the Wrong Feeling.
persona: Haircare founder running a scalp-treatment blog; well-researched post ranks top five, CTR sits at half the category average.
angle: Problem first: a composite founder shows her post is genuinely well-written and well-ranked, but organic CTR undershoots category benchmarks for that position, the content answers the question accurately without giving the searcher a reason to click now. identify_decision_trigger flags momentum as the missing lever: scalp-health searchers are usually mid-frustration and ready to act, and her clinical, patient tone reads as 'read later' instead of 'fix it today.' The hook plays momentum: rewrite the snippet to meet someone already reaching for their card, not someone still researching.

title: I Guessed Which Image Would Win. I Was Wrong.
persona: Home-fragrance founder selling soy candles; gut-picked a 'prettier' main image redesign, CTR dropped after launch.
angle: A composite founder admits she swapped her main image based on instinct and watched CTR drop 15% the following week, a costly guess she could have tested first. The coach runs design_test to turn her hunch into a structured hypothesis (lifestyle shot vs. clean product shot, a defined success metric, a real sample size) instead of a live gamble on ad spend. The hook plays permission: you're allowed to not know which image wins, that's what the test is for.

title: Three Ad Hooks Walked Into a Test. One Won by Miles.
persona: Golf-accessory founder selling a grip trainer; unsure which of three ad angles to scale spend behind.
angle: Review-style, a composite founder walks through the design_test setup the coach built for her three ad hooks: one on recognition (fix your slice), one on identity (play like you practice), one on belonging (what your foursome's been quietly using), each with a defined sample and a single CTR success metric. The hook plays momentum: instead of guessing which trigger to scale, she had a number to scale toward within 48 hours.

title: We A/B Tested a Meta Description. CTR Jumped.
persona: Automotive-accessory founder selling a dash cam; page-one ranking, mediocre organic CTR, unsure if the fix is content or copy.
angle: Screen-share, a composite founder runs two meta description variants through design_test, one leading with 'best dash cam' features, one leading with the specific fear (the accident you couldn't prove wasn't your fault), with a defined test window and click-through as the single metric. The hook plays fear_of_loss: the variant naming the exact bad day the searcher is trying to avoid won by a wide margin.

title: Ranking Well, Clicked Rarely, So We Tested the Title
persona: Bakeware founder running a blog post on 'best silicone bakeware for beginners'; solid ranking, CTR stuck below benchmark for eight weeks.
angle: Problem first: a composite founder shows eight flat weeks of the same underperforming CTR on a well-ranked post, having already rewritten the intro twice with no change. The coach runs design_test on the one variable she hadn't touched, the title itself, structuring a hypothesis (benefit-led vs. keyword-led) with a clear before/after CTR comparison instead of another guess-and-republish cycle. The hook plays momentum: the test took an afternoon, the two rewrites had taken two months.

title: I Stopped Rewriting and Started Testing
persona: Wellness-brand founder selling a cork yoga mat; a content refresh cycle of rewriting the same post every quarter with no CTR movement.
angle: A composite founder narrates her old habit: every quarter, rewrite the post, hope for better clicks, watch the number stay flat, repeat. The coach introduces design_test as the missing step, turning 'let's try a new title' into an actual hypothesis with a defined metric and a real comparison window, so she finally knows whether a change worked instead of just feeling busy. The hook plays permission: stopping the rewrite treadmill isn't giving up, it's finally measuring.

title: Our Video Ad Had Great Views. Nobody Clicked Through.
persona: Men's-grooming founder selling beard oil; a UGC-style video ad racked up views and comments, click-through rate near zero.
angle: Review-style, a composite founder explains her video was genuinely entertaining, good watch time, good comments, but never told anyone what to click or why now, because it was built as content, not as a directed ad. The coach runs generate_video_storyboard to rebuild it scene-by-scene with a specific hook, a claim-gated talking point, and an actual CTA beat instead of just a vibe. The hook plays momentum: entertainment without direction is a views chart, not a business result.

title: We Storyboarded the Ad Before Filming a Single Second
persona: Kids'-education founder selling phonics flashcards; limited budget for a video shoot, one chance to get the ad right.
angle: Screen-recording, a composite founder walks through generate_video_storyboard's scene-by-scene plan before booking a single hour of filming: hook, three trigger-angled openings to test, and a defined CTA beat, all mapped before spending on production. The hook plays permission: she doesn't need three video shoots to find what works, she needs one shoot built around a tested plan.

title: Our YouTube Video Ranks. Almost Nobody Clicks the Thumbnail.
persona: Camping-gear founder selling a portable water filter; a how-to video ranks on page one of YouTube search, click-through rate far below the video's rank would suggest.
angle: Problem first: a composite founder shows her how-to video sitting in a strong YouTube search position with an anemic click-through rate, because the thumbnail and title were an afterthought bolted on after editing, not planned as part of the pitch. The coach runs generate_video_storyboard to rebuild the opening scene and on-screen text specifically for the thumbnail-and-title moment, treating the first three seconds as seo_content real estate, not just video content. The hook plays fear_of_loss: the exact scenario campers search for (filter fails, no clean water, hours from the car) becomes the thumbnail's implied stakes instead of a generic product shot.

title: I Made a Video for Google. Google Rewarded the Wrong One.
persona: Phone-accessory founder selling a magnetic car mount; two videos embedded in the same buying-guide post, the weaker one getting all the clicks.
angle: A composite founder describes the irony: her more polished video sits ignored while a rougher, more specific one pulls almost all the click volume, because the specific one opens on the exact problem ('phone falling off the dash on a pothole') and the polished one opens on the brand logo. The coach runs generate_video_storyboard to rebuild the polished video's opening scene around that same specific problem instead of the brand intro. The hook plays recognition: viewers click the video that opens on their exact annoyance, not the nicest one.

title: The Review Video Got Views. The Blog Post Got Ignored.
persona: Food-brand founder selling a protein snack bar; an embedded review-style video performs well, but the surrounding blog post's organic CTR stays flat.
angle: Review-style, a composite founder notes her embedded UGC review video gets solid watch time, but the actual search snippet driving people to the page barely moves, because the video and the page's SEO hook were built separately and don't reinforce each other. generate_video_storyboard's storyboard-image mode gets used to align the video's opening beat with the exact phrase the page ranks for, so the search snippet and the video promise the same specific payoff. The hook plays momentum: one clear promise repeated at both entry points, instead of two half-hooks.

title: 1,200 Sessions, 9 Orders: My Kitchen Listing's Trust Gap
persona: Founder of a stand-mixer-attachment kitchen brand watching sessions spike from a viral video while orders stay flat.
angle: Sessions tripled overnight after a viral clip; orders didn't move, and she assumed it was price. run_trust_gap scans her amazon_listing_copy and names Authentic as the weakest pillar — the bullets read like a spec sheet, not like a real cook. Hook trigger: recognition — you already sense something's off, you just haven't been told what.

title: My Supplement Brand Story Page Was Lying by Omission
persona: Founder of a daily-greens supplement brand whose A+ content reads like a lab report nobody asked for.
angle: He built the brand story to sound clinical and credible, proud of the polish. run_trust_gap flags Empathetic as the weakest pillar on his amazon_brand_story module — nobody there sounds like they've actually tasted the product. Hook trigger: identity — does this page sound like the founder, or like a compliance form?

title: 4 Stars, Still No Repeat Buyers — the Trust Gap Test
persona: Founder of a dog joint-supplement brand whose reviews look strong but conversion still lags competitors.
angle: A customer reads her own glowing review to camera, proof the reviews look fine on the surface. But the founder's run_trust_gap scan of displayed_reviews finds they all answer 'does it work' and never the real fear — 'will my dog actually eat it.' Hook trigger: fear_of_loss — the review section is confirming the wrong worry.

title: Why 4.6 Stars Isn't Saving My Baby Carrier's Conversion
persona: Founder of an ergonomic baby-carrier brand whose star rating is strong but sessions-to-orders keeps slipping.
angle: She assumed a high star average would carry the listing on its own. run_trust_gap on displayed_reviews shows Insight-Driven scoring lowest — the visible reviews never touch the safety-anxiety question new parents type first. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss — parents scroll reviews hunting for the thing that could go wrong.

title: I Rewrote My Brand Store 'About' Page 6 Times. Still Flat.
persona: Founder of a clean-skincare line whose Brand Store About section keeps getting rewritten with no lift.
angle: Every rewrite made the About page more polished and less true, and nothing moved. run_trust_gap on brand_store_about names Authentic as the score dragging everything down — the polish was the problem. Hook trigger: identity — the version that read best on paper converted worst.

title: The One Page on My Brand Store Nobody Actually Trusts
persona: Founder of a camping-gear brand whose Brand Store gets browsed but never converts to a purchase.
angle: Traffic hits the Brand Store, wanders the About page, and leaves without buying. run_trust_gap isolates Distinctive as the weak pillar — the page describes 'quality outdoor gear' the same way eleven competitors do. Hook trigger: belonging — nothing tells this specific camper they're the right customer.

title: Why My Coffee Brand's 'About' Page Reads Like Everyone Else's
persona: Single-origin coffee roaster whose Brand Store About page gets views but zero click-through to the listing.
angle: Brand Store visits climb; listing click-throughs stay at zero. On screen, run_trust_gap flags the about section's low Distinctive score, then directs the fix — turning 'ethically sourced' filler into his real origin story. Hook trigger: momentum — a click-through problem framed as one fixable score.

title: My Buy Box Has Zero Trust Signals and I Never Noticed
persona: Founder of a resistance-bands fitness brand losing shoppers right at the buy box despite decent reviews.
angle: A customer admits on camera she almost skipped buying because she couldn't tell if the brand was legit. run_trust_gap later confirms that exact drop-off at trust_badges_social_proof — no certification or guarantee near the buy button. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss.

title: The Buy Box Gap Nobody Told Me About My Chair Pad Listing
persona: Founder of an ergonomic seat-cushion brand for home offices watching add-to-cart rates fall right before checkout.
angle: Add-to-carts look fine; checkouts don't follow. run_trust_gap on trust_badges_social_proof shows no return-policy or material-safety cue near the buy box — the missing permission signal. Hook trigger: permission — shoppers need something that says it's safe to click buy.

title: I Found the Exact Line Shoppers Read Before Abandoning Cart
persona: Founder of a garage tool-organizer brand who kept losing shoppers between add-to-cart and purchase.
angle: He blamed price for a month before looking anywhere else. run_trust_gap traces the abandon point to trust_badges_social_proof — no warranty language where every competitor has one. Hook trigger: momentum — naming the exact stalling sentence turns despair into a to-do list.

title: My Gallery Images Show the Product. Never the Feeling.
persona: Founder of a women's activewear brand whose gallery is all flat-lay shots and spec close-ups.
angle: Filming her own try-on in classic UGC style, she realizes on camera her gallery has no 'felt moment' — no lifestyle shot of a real body wearing it under pressure. generate_listing_image_brief hands back that exact slot, spec'd for amazon_listing_copy. Hook trigger: identity — she can't see herself in her own gallery.

title: Nobody Buys a Craft Kit From a Photo of Just the Kit
persona: Founder of a resin-craft starter-kit brand whose main image is a flat-lay of components with no finished piece visible.
angle: A customer admits she almost skipped the listing because she couldn't picture what she'd end up making. generate_listing_image_brief adds the missing gallery slot to amazon_listing_copy — a finished-piece felt-moment shot that answers exactly that. Hook trigger: recognition.

title: My STEM Toy's Photos Sell Parts. Parents Buy Outcomes.
persona: Founder of a STEM-building-block toy brand whose gallery is dominated by piece-count infographics.
angle: Parents scroll six slides of parts and dimensions looking for one answer: will my kid actually play with this. generate_listing_image_brief restructures the amazon_listing_copy gallery order around the one lifestyle image that answers it, moved earlier. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss — the fear of buying another toy that gets ignored.

title: I Had Nine Product Shots and Not One Human in Them
persona: Founder of a weighted-blanket sleep brand whose entire gallery is texture close-ups and folded-product shots.
angle: She built the gallery like a fabric swatch book, proud of the materials. generate_listing_image_brief's brief for amazon_listing_copy names the missing slot: someone actually asleep under it. Hook trigger: belonging — customers need to see someone like them getting the relief.

title: The One Gallery Slot Every Candle Listing Is Missing
persona: Founder of a home-fragrance candle brand whose gallery has scent notes and jar dimensions but no room context.
angle: Screen-record style: the coach walks the exact gallery brief generate_listing_image_brief produces for amazon_listing_copy, pointing to the lifestyle slot most candle brands skip. Hook trigger: momentum — a specific slot to fill today, not a redesign.

title: I Unboxed My Own A+ Content Before Publishing It
persona: Founder of a skincare gift-set brand about to publish A+ content built from a generic template.
angle: Instead of unboxing product, she unboxes the printed mock-up generate_aplus_content_plan produced, revealing beat by beat where her amazon_brand_story module just repeats the bullet points. Hook trigger: recognition — the 'oh, it really is just the bullets again' moment.

title: My A+ Content Has Five Slides. Zero New Reasons to Buy.
persona: Founder of a ceramic-cookware brand whose A+ content restates the listing bullets in bigger fonts.
angle: A shopper says on camera she scrolled the whole brand story and learned nothing new. generate_aplus_content_plan's 5-beat structure gives the founder new addressable content for amazon_brand_story that the old page never had. Hook trigger: recognition.

title: Why My Cat Litter Brand's A+ Content Doesn't Move Anyone
persona: Founder of a clumping cat-litter brand whose A+ content stacks the same odor-control claim three times.
angle: Three beats, one claim, restated. generate_aplus_content_plan's 5 addressable beats force amazon_brand_story onto the real objection — multi-cat households worried about tracking — that the old page never touched. Hook trigger: identity — speaking to the specific household, not 'cat owners.'

title: I Thought My Brand Story Needed Better Photos. It Needed Beats.
persona: Founder of an electrolyte-mix supplement brand convinced her A+ content just needed a photography upgrade.
angle: New photos, same structure, same flat conversion. generate_aplus_content_plan reframes amazon_brand_story as one continuous editorial composition across 5 addressable beats instead of five disconnected banners. Hook trigger: momentum — the fix wasn't visual, it was structural.

title: One Continuous Story Beats Five Random Banners
persona: Founder of an insulated-hydration-bottle brand whose A+ content is five unrelated promotional banners.
angle: Screen capture of generate_aplus_content_plan turning five disconnected banners into one scrolling 1472x3008 composition for amazon_brand_story with a beginning, middle, and end. Hook trigger: momentum.

title: The Reviews Were Warning Me About Shade Match. I Wasn't Reading.
persona: Founder of a base-shade makeup brand getting quiet shade-accuracy complaints buried in her reviews.
angle: A customer films a shade-match try-on and mentions the photos looked lighter than this in person — the exact complaint pattern ingest_evidence surfaces when it parses displayed_reviews for recurring language. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss — shoppers share the same fear and the page never answers it.

title: I Ingested My Own Reviews and Found the Real Objection
persona: Founder of a scalp-care serum brand who assumed her reviews were just 'generally positive.'
angle: A satisfied customer describes her results on camera — reassuring on the surface. But ingest_evidence parsing displayed_reviews turns up a second pattern: 'took 6 weeks, almost gave up,' an objection the listing never pre-answers. Hook trigger: recognition.

title: My Reviews Kept Mentioning a Problem I Never Fixed
persona: Founder of a silicone teething-toy brand whose reviews repeatedly mention a concern the listing never addresses.
angle: ingest_evidence parses displayed_reviews and surfaces the same safety question about materials nine times over — a question that appears nowhere on the listing. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss.

title: My Listing Copy Never Mentioned the Thing Customers Actually Say
persona: Founder of a cold-brew-concentrate brand whose bullets read like a product spec sheet.
angle: He wrote the bullets straight off the ingredient panel. ingest_evidence parses real reviews and listing snapshots and feeds back the actual vocabulary customers use for amazon_listing_copy — vocabulary his page never once used. Hook trigger: identity — his own customers' words, missing from his own page.

title: Feed the Coach Your Reviews. Watch Your Bullets Get Rewritten.
persona: Founder of a car-detailing-kit brand with generic, benefit-free bullet points.
angle: Screen capture: paste in the ASIN, ingest_evidence parses the review history, and the coach flags which lines in amazon_listing_copy contradict what customers actually praise. Hook trigger: momentum.

title: I Audited My Own Listing Like a Stranger Would Read It
persona: Founder of a non-slip yoga-mat brand who hadn't re-read her own listing copy in over a year.
angle: A first-time shopper reads the listing aloud and stumbles on a line that contradicts the hero image. audit_asset run against amazon_listing_copy flags that exact same mismatch against her customer avatar. Hook trigger: recognition.

title: One Asset, One Avatar, One Verdict: My A+ Content Failed
persona: Founder of a kitchen-knife-set brand who wants one module checked before a relaunch, not a full rebuild.
angle: He doesn't want to redo the whole funnel — just knows something's off with one module. audit_asset scores his amazon_brand_story asset against the customer avatar and returns a specific miss: no beat addresses knife-maintenance anxiety. Hook trigger: permission — you don't need a full audit to get an answer.

title: I Thought My Reviews Section Was Doing Its Job. It Wasn't.
persona: Founder of a grain-free dog-treats brand confident her reviews section was pulling its weight.
angle: She never questioned it — it had stars, it had volume. audit_asset scores that single displayed_reviews asset against her actual customer avatar and comes back low: none of the surfaced reviews speak to allergy-prone dogs, her real worry segment. Hook trigger: identity.

title: My Brand Store 'About' Page Passed Every Vibe Check but One
persona: Founder of an embroidery-starter-kit brand who liked her Brand Store About page but couldn't say why it wasn't converting.
angle: It reads well and looks nice. audit_asset scores brand_store_about against her actual avatar and returns the miss: nothing there tells a total beginner they belong. Hook trigger: belonging.

title: I Unboxed My Own Product and Saw What's Missing at Checkout
persona: Founder of a silicone baby-bib brand reviewing her own packaging on camera before a relaunch.
angle: Filming a routine unboxing for content, she notices there's no visible safety-certification mark. audit_asset scoring trust_badges_social_proof against her avatar confirms it's the exact signal anxious first-time parents search for near the buy box. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss.

title: The Coach Scanned My Funnel. The Listing Was the Weak Link.
persona: Founder of an anti-fatigue standing-desk-mat brand who assumed her ad targeting was the conversion problem.
angle: She blamed the ad campaign for a month. run_funnel_audit overlays her avatar across every touchpoint and names amazon_listing_copy as the single weakest link — everything downstream of the click was fine. Hook trigger: momentum — one weak link, not a whole rebuild.

title: My Funnel Audit Pointed at the One Page I Never Touch
persona: Founder of a manual pour-over coffee-kit brand who hadn't updated his Brand Story module in over a year.
angle: A customer says the ad convinced her, the listing convinced her, then she got to the brand story and just left. run_funnel_audit's weakest-link overlay flags amazon_brand_story exactly there. Hook trigger: recognition.

title: Every Touchpoint Scored Fine Except One. Here's the Overlay.
persona: Founder of a plant-based protein-powder brand running a full-funnel check before a relaunch push.
angle: Screen capture of run_funnel_audit's per-avatar overlay across the funnel, landing on displayed_reviews as the single flagged position — the surfaced reviews don't match what her real avatar segment cares about. Hook trigger: momentum.

title: I Braced for the Listing to Fail the Audit. It Didn't.
persona: Founder of a hiking-backpack brand who assumed her Amazon listing was the problem all along.
angle: She braced for bad news about her bullets. run_funnel_audit clears the listing entirely and instead flags brand_store_about — the Brand Store nobody told her shoppers were visiting before buying. Hook trigger: recognition — the weak link was somewhere she wasn't even looking.

title: The Funnel Audit Found My Buy Box Was the Actual Leak
persona: Founder of a melatonin-free sleep-gummy brand chasing a conversion problem she assumed was pricing.
angle: run_funnel_audit's weakest-link scan clears pricing entirely and flags trust_badges_social_proof — no third-party testing badge where every competitor shows three. Hook trigger: fear_of_loss.

persona: Founder of an essential-oil-diffuser brand who used to fix listing problems with one-line Slack messages to her VA.
angle: A customer describes exactly what confused her about the brand story section on camera. generate_brief turns that finding into a designer-ready spec for amazon_brand_story, handed off the same day. Hook trigger: momentum — a fix that ships today, not eventually.

persona: Founder of a portable-tire-inflator brand who kept meaning to fix the Brand Store About page and never did.

persona: Founder of a candle-making-supplies brand who could feel something was wrong near the buy button but not name it.

persona: Founder of a dog joint-chews brand watching five-star reviews say nothing about the brand.

persona: Founder of a cold-brew coffee bag brand recording another founder-story reel that sounds like everyone else's.

persona: Founder of a soy candle brand whose About page sounds like every candle brand's About page.
